title: The Preaching of Jonah Against Nineveh
introduction:
During the middle of Jeroboam II's reign, around 780-775 BC, one of the most compelling preaching missions in history takes place. God calls Jonah, a man from southern Galilee, to carry a special message of repentance to the great city of Nineveh and to the pagan Assyrians who live there. Nineveh is one of the oldest great cities, founded as early as the patriarchal age by the mighty hunter Nimrod. Located east of the Tigris River, it serves as a royal residence for the kings of Assyria. Jonah's mission falls between the reigns of Shalmaneser III and Tiglath-Pileser III, during the rule of a lesser-known king.

From a human point of view, the task seems almost impossible. That impossibility becomes even clearer when Jonah tries to escape this special responsibility and is swallowed by a great fish miraculously prepared by God for that moment. Convinced of God's unlimited power, Jonah goes to deliver the message that Nineveh is about to be destroyed and that repentance is urgently needed. To his surprise, and even disappointment, the people of Nineveh repent and are spared from imminent destruction.

Taken as a whole, the story powerfully displays God's universal grace and divine concern. The Israelites, who often tended toward exclusivism, needed to be reminded that even though they were God's chosen people, without repentance their spiritual condition could fall below that of repentant pagans.

sections.title: In Israel
sections.introduction: The historical record now confirms Jonah's mission and tells how God gives Jeroboam great military success and prosperity.
